text: The surgeon places a Lone Star retractor to expose the surgical field and infiltrates the vaginal wall with an adrenaline solution. A seton is visible at the start of the procedure.

The surgeon removes the ceton and inserts a fistula probe, injecting methylene blue into the fistula track. Stay sutures are placed above the fistula track, with the suture material hooked around the lone star retractor to enhance exposure. The third suture is carefully placed and secured around the retractor.

Three sutures are placed inferior to the vaginal opening of the fistula tract to enhance exposure. Sharp dissection is performed around the vaginal opening with the fistula probe in position, facilitating movement and exposure of the fistula tract for dissection and removal.

The surgeon uses a clip on the fistula track to aid in dissection, pulling on the track to sharply dissect down to the pouch opening of the fistula. The fistula track is visibly short, which is demonstrated during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a suction device to define the edges of the fistula openings before closing the internal opening on the pouch side with dissolvable monofilament sutures. A finger is placed in the rectum to aid in exposing and defining the internal opening of the fistula. The fistula is located at the site of the previous staple anastomosis, which may have contributed to the formation of the fistula.

The surgeon defines the tissues on the vaginal side of the fistula, encountering difficulty on the left side due to the presence of a previous Martius flap. Some remaining tissues of the Martius flap are visible and are freed to cover the internal opening of the fistula. On the right side of the vagina, the tissues appear more pliable as no Martius flap was raised there.

The surgeon frees up the vaginal musculature and attached levators to provide adequate muscle tissue for burying and closing the internal opening of the fistula tract. The vaginal mucosa is elevated further with the insertion of local anesthetic.

The surgical field shows relatively bloody tissues as the dissection continues in a cephalic direction. The surgeon uses suction to maintain a clear view and good lighting to aid in the dissection. The vaginal musculature is being fully mobilized to ensure the interlobe of the fistula is closed effectively.

The surgeon places a suture at the top end of the dissection to bring muscle across and close the upper aspect of the fistula dissection. Further dissection of the left Martius flap, visible as yellow tissue in the center, is used to suture over the internal defect and close the internal opening of the fistula. The vaginal musculature is examined before inserting sutures to bring the two sides together, ensuring well-nourished, vascularized muscle covers the previous site of the tract.

The surgeon uses a pyretractor to expose the vault of the vagina, ensuring the vaginal mucosa is closed along its length to bury the muscular repair. Monofilament suture is applied to minimize infection risk.

The surgeon closes the vaginal mucosa with monofilament sutures, completing the dissection. A urinary catheter is inserted, and a vaginal pack soaked in estrogen cream is placed to conclude the operation.
